content:
The parts of a demo to skip and how to build them.

9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You can build an RAG demo in an afternoon. Point a model at your documents, ask a question, and get an answer with a citation. Then real users arrive, and it starts failing. It cites the wrong paragraph, misses an exact error code, or provides answers from a document that does not exist.
The model is usually fine. It writes good answers when the evidence is good. The failures stem from the retrieval around it, and a demo has almost none of that retrieval built in.
A demo is five steps:

• Split documents into chunks.

• Embed each chunk.

• Store the embeddings in a vector database.

• Retrieve the chunks closest to the query.

• Put them in the prompt and generate.
This works for you and breaks for users. A production system adds document preparation, metadata, hybrid retrieval, reranking, grounding, access control, evaluation, caching, and monitoring.
Microsoft’s RAG guidance treats each of these as a separate decision, depending on the workload. The diagram below separates the two. The pipeline is the inner line. The system is everything around it.
